data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "The Sea Peoples were not merely raiders but symptom-bearers of a systemic maritime-trade network collapse that cascaded through palace economies dependent on bulk bronze and luxury goods circulation", "mechanism": "Maritime supply-chain fragmentation: the Late Bronze Age Mediterranean was integrated by naval technology and harbor infrastructure that created network externalities. When piracy/raiding crossed a threshold (~LH IIIB2), insurance costs and route abandonment triggered positive feedback—palaces lost tin (Cornwall/Afghanistan) and copper (Cyprus) access simultaneously, making bronze production impossible; gift-exchange diplomacies failed without circulating prestige goods, delegitimizing rulers; Egypt survived because its Nile corridor permitted internal redistribution and Nubian gold substituted for trade income", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states", "Hittite Anatolia (western provinces)", "Cyprus"], "excludes": ["Egypt proper", "Mesopotamia", "central Anatolian highlands post-1180"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Sea Peoples activity attested before palace collapse at all sites", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Egyptian trade dependence equal to Mycenaean palaces", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Cyprus copper production ceased before 1200", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Central Anatolia collapse simultaneous with coast", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Emphasizes structural vulnerability of thalassocratic networks; Egypt's riparian geography as exogenous survival factor. Ref: school-0 mech stance."}
